content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

May 12, 2016

Albert S. Yang
Senior Deputy City Attorney
Office of the City Attorney
250 Hamilton Avenue, 8th Fl.
Palo Alto, CA 94301

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-16-082

Dear Mr. Yang:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Palo Alto City Councilmember
Liz Kniss, City Councilmember Greg Schmid, and Senior Assistant City Attorney Cara Silver
regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Because the
Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when
rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71), this letter is based on the facts presented.

QUESTIONS

Assuming Section 87100 prohibits the following public officials from taking part in the
making of the following governmental decisions:

1. May Councilmember Kniss take part in decisions relating to the establishment of a
Residential Preferential Parking District ("RPP District") in the Evergreen Park neighborhood
pursuant to the "limited neighborhood effects" exception?

2. May Councilmember Schmid take part in decisions relating to the creation of a Single
Story Overlay District ("SSO District") in the Royal Manor neighborhood pursuant to the
exception?

3. May Senior Assistant City Attorney Silver take part in decisions relating to the Concept
Plan for Bicycle and Pedestrian Improvements Along Amarillo Avenue, Bryant Street, East
Meadow Drive, Montrose Avenue, Moreno Avenue, Louis Road, Palo Alto Avenue, and Ross Road
("Concept Plan") pursuant to the exception?

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-16-082
Page No. 2

CONCLUSIONS

1. No. Councilmember Kniss may not invoke the "limited neighborhood effects" exception
to take part in decisions relating to the establishment of a RPP District in the Evergreen Park
neighborhood because the decision could have a unique effect on the Councilmember's interest in a
6-unit multi-family property within the proposed RPP District.

2. No. Councilmember Schmid may not invoke the exception to take part in decisions
relating to the creation of a SSO District in the Royal Manor neighborhood because the creation of a
SSO District is not an action authorized under the exception.

3. No. Senior Assistant City Attorney Silver may not invoke the exception to take part in
decisions relating to the Concept Plan because the decision generally affects various types of real
property throughout the City, and you have not sufficiently evidenced a need for action at any
"specific location."

FACTS

You are a Senior Deputy City Attorney for the City of Palo Alto and the authorized
representative of City Councilmember Liz Kniss, City Councilmember Greg Schmid, and Senior
Assistant City Attorney Cara Silver.

Councilmember Kniss and the RPP District in the Evergreen Park Neighborhood:

On May 9, 2016, the City Council considered whether to establish a RPP District in the
Evergreen Park neighborhood, and Councilmember Kniss recused herself from that decision.
Because the City Council may make further decisions regarding the Evergreen Park RPP District in
the near future, you have requested that we provide advice on this question. You ask us to assume
that Section 87100 prohibited Councilmember Kniss from taking part in these decisions.

In early 2015, the City Council adopted an ordinance setting forth criteria and procedures
for the establishment of RPP Districts within the City. Under the ordinance, a RPP District may be
proposed by either the City Council or by neighborhood petition on a standard form application
established by the City's Director of Planning and Community Environment. (PAMC Sections
10.50.040 and 10.50.050.) The ordinance authorizes the City Council to designate an area as a RPP
District if certain criteria are met. (PAMC Section 10.50.030.)

Recently, both Evergreen Park residents and City Councilmembers have expressed interest
in establishing a RPP district in the neighborhood, citing parking intrusion from the California
Avenue commercial area and Stanford University. In the summer of 2015, concerned Evergreen
Park residents gathered over 225 signatures (from 300 units surveyed in the neighborhood)
requesting that a RPP District be established in the neighborhood.

It is unclear at this time to what extent parking would be restricted in the Evergreen Park
neighborhood if the City establishes an RPP district in the neighborhood. The City Council will
design parking restrictions appropriate to the RPP District if it is established. In establishing the

File No. A-16-082
Page No. 3

Downtown RPP District, the City awarded one free parking permit to each dwelling unit in the
District and allowed the occupants of each dwelling unit to purchase up to three additional permits.

Councilmember Kniss has an ownership interest in a 6-unit multi-family residential real
property in the Evergreen Park neighborhood, and she is not a resident of that property.

Councilmember Schmid and the SSO District in the Royal Manor Neighborhood:

On May 9, 2016, the City Council considered whether to establish a SSO District in the
Royal Manor neighborhood, and Councilmember Schmid recused himself from that decision.
Because the City Council may make further decisions regarding the implementation of the SSO
District in the near future, you have requested that we provide advice on this question. You ask
to assume that Section 87100 prohibited Councilmember Schmid from taking part in these
decisions.

The Royal Manor neighborhood contains approximately 200 homes. If a SSO District is
created in the Royal Manor neighborhood, a maximum height of 20 feet and a limit of one
inhabitable floor would be imposed on buildings within the SSO District. (PAMC Section
18.12.100). In adopting SSO Districts for similar neighborhoods, the City Council has found that a
SSO District "is in the public interest and will promote the public health, safety, and welfare."
However, no other facts have been presented indicating that the creation of the SSO District will
promote public safety.

Councilmember Schmid has a residential real property interest in the Royal Manor
neighborhood that would be subject to the SSO District if the City Council creates it.

Senior Assistant City Attorney Silver and the Concept Plan for further Bike Boulevards:

On May 9, 2016, the City Council considered whether to approve the Concept Plan, and
Senior Assistant City Attorney Siler recused herself from taking part in that decision. Because the
City Council may make further decisions regarding the implementation of the Concept Plan in the
near future, you have requested that we provide advice on this question. You ask us to assume that
Section 87100 prohibited Senior Assistant City Attorney Silver from taking part in these decisions.

The decision at issue would, among other things, establish or expand Bike Boulevards in
different areas of the City toward the overarching goal of creating a network of Bike Boulevards
throughout the City. Specifically, the decision would advance four particular projects in different
areas of the City, the Amarillo Avenue-Moreno Avenue, Bryant Street Update, Louis Road-
Montrose Avenue, and Ross Road Bike Boulevard projects. The proposed project would affect
various types of real property throughout the City, and no facts have been presented indicating that
the Concept Plan is necessary in any specific location.

Senior Assistant City Attorney Silver has an ownership interest in real property along the
proposed Amarillo-Moreno Avenue Bike Boulevard project.

File No. A-16-082
Page No. 4

ANALYSIS

The Act's conflict of interest provisions ensure that public officials will perform their duties
in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the financial
interests of persons who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).)

Section 87100 prohibits a public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise
using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a
financial interest. Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial interest" in a
governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect on one or more of the official's interests, and describes interests from which a conflict of
interest may arise under the Act. Thus, with respect to conflicts of interest under the Act, the
general rule is that a prohibited conflict of interest exists whenever a public official makes a
governmental decision that has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally, on one or more of the official's financial interests. (Sections
87100 and 87103; Regulation 18700(a).)

Regulation 18703(e)(3) sets forth the "limited neighborhood effects" exception, a specific
rule for special circumstances associated with the Act's "public generally" exception. You ask
whether the exception applies to the decisions at issue. You have asked us to assume that a conflict
of interest exists under the Act with respect to each question presented.

Exceptions to the general rule of a statute are strictly construed. (People v. Melton (1988)
206 Cal.App.3d 580, 592; City of National City v. Fritz (1949) 33 Cal.2d 635, 636.) Regulation
18703(e)(3) provides as follows:

"(e) Specific Rules for Special Circumstances. The financial effect on a public
official's financial interest is deemed indistinguishable from that of the public
generally if the official establishes: …

"(3) Limited Neighborhood Effects. The decision affects residential real
property limited to a specific location, and the decision establishes, amends, or
eliminates ordinances that restrict on-street parking, impose traffic controls,
deter vagrancy, reduce nuisance or improve public safety, provided the body
making the decision gathers sufficient evidence to support the need for the
action at the specific location."

Thus, under the "limited neighborhood effects" exception, the "financial effect on a public official's
financial interest is deemed indistinguishable from that of the public generally" if the official
establishes: (1) the decision affects residential real property limited to a specific location; (2) the
decision establishes, amends, or eliminates ordinances that restrict on-street parking, impose traffic
controls, deter vagrancy, reduce nuisance, or improve public safety; and (3) the body making the
decision has gathered sufficient evidence to support the need for the action at the specific location.

File No. A-16-082
Page No. 5

Councilmember Kniss and the RPP District in the Evergreen Park Neighborhood:

We now consider whether Councilmember Kniss may take part in decisions relating to the establishment of a RPP District in the Evergreen Park neighborhood pursuant to the "limited neighborhood effects" exception.

In order for a public official to invoke the "limited neighborhood effects" exception, the action at issue must not have a unique financial effect on the official's interests. (Beryl Advice Letter, No. I-15-196; See also Section 87103 and Regulation 18703(c).) Based on the facts presented, Councilmember Kniss has an ownership interest in a 6-unit multi-family residential real property in the Evergreen Park neighborhood. The financial effect of the establishment of the Evergreen Park RPP District on the value of a 6-unit multi-family property within the District is "substantially greater" than the financial effect on the value of a single-family property within the District, as a parking permit for each dwelling unit within a RPP District would confer six times greater aggregate benefit to the owner of a 6-unit multi-family property than the owner of a single-family property. (See Regulation 18703(e)(3).) Because the establishing of the Evergreen Park RPP District could result in a "unique effect" on Councilmember Kniss' interest in her 6-unit multi-family property, the exception does not apply.

Thus, we conclude that Councilmember Kniss may not invoke the "limited neighborhood effects" exception to take part in decisions relating to establishment of a RPP District in the Evergreen Park neighborhood.

Councilmember Schmid and the SSO District in the Royal Manor Neighborhood:

We next consider whether Councilmember Schmid may take part in decisions relating to the creation of a SSO District for the Royal Manor neighborhood pursuant to the "limited neighborhood effects" exception.

The decisions at issue involve the creation of a SSO District in the Royal Manor neighborhood. Therefore, the decision affects residential real property limited to a specific location, and the first element of the exception is met.

The facts presented note that the City Council, in creating SSO Districts in similar neighborhoods in the past, has found that a SSO District is "in the public interest and will promote the public health, safety, and welfare." A mere finding that an action will promote the public safety, however, is not dispositive of the question. The facts presented provide no evidence supporting the conclusion that the decision would "restrict on-street parking, impose traffic controls, deter vagrancy, reduce nuisance or improve public safety" as required by the regulation. Therefore, we find that the second element of the exception is not satisfied.

Accordingly, we conclude that Councilmember Schmid may not invoke the "limited neighborhood effects" exception to take part in decisions relating to the creation of a SSO District in the Royal Manor neighborhood.

File No. A-16-082
Page No. 6

Senior Assistant City Attorney Silver and the Concept Plan for further Bike Boulevards:

Finally, we consider whether Senior Assistant Attorney Silver may take part in decisions relating to the Concept Plan to establish or expand Bike Boulevards throughout the City pursuant to the "limited neighborhood effects" exception.

The decisions at issue would, among other things, establish or expand Bike Boulevards in four different areas of the City, toward achievement of the overarching goal of creating a network of Bike Boulevards throughout the City. Specifically, the decisions would advance four particular Bike Boulevard projects, the Amarillo Avenue-Moreno Avenue, Bryant Street Update, Louis Road-Montrose Avenue, and Ross Road Bike Boulevard projects. Based upon the facts provided, the decisions generally affect various types of real property throughout the City, and you have not sufficiently evidenced a need for action at any "specific location."

Thus, we conclude that Senior Assistant City Attorney Silver may not invoke the "limited neighborhood effects" exception to take part in decisions relating to the Concept Plan.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: Matthew F. Christy
Counsel, Legal Division

MFC:jgl

Assuming Section 87100 prohibits the following public officials from taking part in the

Story Overlay District ("SSO District") in the Royal Manor neighborhood pursuant to the 
exception? 
3. May Senior Assistant City Attorney Silver take part in decisions relating to the Concept 
Plan for Bicycle and Pedestrian Improvements Along Amarillo A venue, Bryant Street, East 
Meadow Drive, Montrose A venue, Moreno A venue, Louis Road, Palo Alto A venue, and Ross Road 
("Concept Plan") pursuant to the exception?

sections.analysis:
The Act's conflict of interest provisions ensure that public officials will perfonn their duties 
in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the financial 
interests of persons who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).) 
Section 87100 prohibits a public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise 
using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a 
financial interest. Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial interest" in a 
governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial

general rule is that a prohibited conflict of interest exists whenever a public official makes a 
governmental decision that has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect, distinguishable 
from its effect on the public generally, on one or more of the official's financial interests. (Sections 
87100 and 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) 
Regulation 18703(e)(3) sets forth the "limited neighborhood effects" exception, a specific 
rule for special circumstances associated with the Act's "public generally" exception. You ask 
whether the exception applies to the decisions at issue. You have asked us to assume that a conflict 
of interest exists under the Act with respect to each question presented. 
Exceptions to the general rule of a statute are strictly construed. (People v. Melton ( 1988) 
206 Cal.App.3d 580,592; City of National City v. Fritz (1949) 33 Cal.2d 635, 636.) Regulation 
18703( e )(3) provides as follows: 
"(e) Specific Rules for Special Circumstances. The financial effect on a public 
official's financial interest is deemed indistinguishable from that of the public 
generally if the official establishes: ... 
"(3) Limited Neighborhood Effects. The decision affects residential real 
property limited to a specific location, and the decision establishes, amends, or 
eliminates ordinances that restrict on-street parking, impose traffic controls, 
deter vagrancy, reduce nuisance or improve public safety, provided the body 
making the decision gathers sufficient evidence to support the need for the 
action at the specific location." 
Thus, under the "limited neighborhood effects" exception, the "financial effect on a public official's 
financial interest is deemed indistinguishable from that of the public generally" if the official 
establishes: ( 1) the decision affects residential real property limited to a specific location; (2) the 
decision establishes, amends, or eliminates ordinances that restrict on-street parking, impose traffic 
controls, deter vagrancy, reduce nuisance, or improve public safety; and (3) the body making the 
decision has gathered sufficient evidence to support the need for the action at the specific location. 


Councilmember Kniss and the RPP District in the Evergreen Park Neighborhood:  
We now consider whether Councilmember Kniss may take part in decisions relating to the 
establishment of a RPP District in the Evergreen Park neighborhood pursuant to the "limited 
neighborhood effects" exception. 
In order for a public official to invoke the "limited neighborhood effects" exception, the 
action at issue must not have a unique financial effect on the official's interests. (Be,yt Advice 
Letter, No. 1-15-196; See also Section 87103 and Regulation 18703(c).) Based on the facts 
presented, Councilmember Kniss has an ownership interest in a 6-unit multi-family residential real 
property in the Evergreen Park neighborhood. The financial effect of the establishment of the 
Evergreen Park RPP District on the value of a 6-unit multi-family property within the District is 
"substantially greater" than the financial effect on the value of a single-family property within the 
District, as a parking permit for each dwelling unit within a RPP District would confer six times 
greater aggregate benefit to the owner of a 6-unit multi-family property than the owner of a single­
family property. (See Regulation 18703(c)(3).) Because the establishment of the Evergreen Park 
RPP District could result in a "unique effect" on Councilmember Kniss' interest in her 6-unit multi­
family property, the exception does not apply. 
Thus, we conclude that Councilmember Kniss may not invoke the "limited neighborhood 
effects" exception to take part in decisions relating to the establishment of a RPP District in the 
Evergreen Park neighborhood. 
Councilmember Schmid and the SSO District in the Royal Manor Neighborhood: 
We next consider whether Councilmember Schmid may take part in decisions relating to the 
creation of a SSO District for the Royal Manor neighborhood pursuant to the "limited neighborhood 
effects" exception. 
The decisions at issue involve the creation of a SSO District in the Royal Manor 
neighborhood. Therefore, the decision affects residential real property limited to a specific location, 
and the first element of the exception is met. 
The facts presented note that the City Council, in creating SSO Districts in similar 
neighborhoods in the past, has found that a SSO District is "in the public interest and will promote 
the public health, safety, and welfare." A mere finding that an action will promote the public safety, 
however, is not dispositive of the question. The facts presented provide no evidence supporting the 
conclusion that the decision would "restrict on-street parking, impose traffic controls, deter 
vagrancy, reduce nuisance or improve public safety" as required by the regulation. Therefore, we 
find that the second element of the exception is not satisfied. 
Accordingly, we conclude that Councilmember Schmid may not invoke the "limited 
neighborhood effects" exception to take part in decisions relating to the creation of a SSO District 
in the Royal Manor neighborhood.  
Senior Assistant City Attorney Silver and the Concept Plan for further Bike Boulevards: 
Finally, we consider whether Senior Assistant Attorney Silver may take part in decisions 
relating to the Concept Plan to establish or expand Bike Boulevards throughout the City pursuant to 
the "limited neighborhood effects" exception. 
The deciqions at issue would, among other things, establish or expand Bike Boulevards in 
four different areas of the City, toward achievement of the overarching goal of creating a network 
of Bike Boulevards throughout the City. Specifically, the decisions would advance four particular

Montrose Avenue, and Ross Road Bike Boulevard projects. Based upon the facts provided, the 
decisions generally affect various types of real property throughout the City, and you have not 
sufficiently evidenced a need for action at any "specific location." 
Thus, we conclude that Senior Assistant City Attorney Silver may not invoke the "limited 
neighborhood effects" exception to take part in decisions relating to the Concept Plan.
